content.text: What It Is

A system prompt style guide defines the structure, voice, and content rules for the instruction text that an AI application sends in the system turn of every conversation. The system prompt is the most powerful single lever available to an AI application developer: it establishes the model's identity, scope, capabilities, constraints, and expected output format before the user's first message. A well-written system prompt produces consistent, safe, and useful outputs. A poorly written one produces unpredictable behaviour, opens safety vulnerabilities, and makes the model's outputs difficult to interpret.

The style guide addresses three concerns: structure (what sections a system prompt must contain and in what order), writing style (active vs passive voice, specific vs vague language, how to write prohibitions), and anti-patterns (constructions that produce unreliable or exploitable behaviour). The guide is enforced through a combination of a linter that checks prompts at review time and a review checklist that human reviewers complete before a template is approved for publication.
Why It Matters

System prompt quality is inversely correlated with incident rate. The most common enterprise AI incidents — scope creep where the model performs tasks outside its intended function, safety bypasses where adversarial users extract prohibited content, and inconsistent output formats that break downstream rendering — are frequently traceable to specific style failures: capability sections that say 'help with anything the user needs' instead of listing bounded capabilities, constraint sections written in passive voice ('harmful content should be avoided') instead of imperative ('never produce content that'), and missing output format sections that leave the model to choose its own response structure.

A style guide also reduces the cognitive load on system prompt authors. Without a canonical structure, authors face a blank page and make arbitrary choices about what to include and where. A five-section canonical structure transforms the authoring task from creative writing to structured form-filling, which is faster, more consistent, and easier to review.

The linter operationalises the style guide. Rather than relying on reviewers to remember every rule, the linter checks for required sections, prohibited language patterns, passive voice constructions, and sycophantic openers automatically. This frees human reviewers to focus on semantic correctness — does the capabilities section accurately describe what the model can do? — rather than syntactic compliance.
Benefits
Predictable output structure.

A mandatory output format section means the model always knows its expected response length, markdown usage rules, and citation format. This reduces the variance in output structure that makes downstream parsing unreliable.
Reduced safety incidents.

Mandatory constraints sections with imperative-voice prohibitions produce more reliable refusal behaviour than passive-voice guidance. The model attends to 'never reveal' instructions more consistently than 'you should avoid revealing' instructions.
Faster authoring and review.
The canonical five-section structure with templates reduces first-draft authoring time and gives reviewers a clear mental model of what to check in each section.
Automated compliance checking.
The system prompt linter catches the most common style violations before human review, reducing review cycles and ensuring baseline compliance even for teams unfamiliar with the style guide.
Consistent brand voice.

Writing style rules — active voice, no sycophantic affirmations, specific over vague — produce a consistent tone across all AI touchpoints in a product, regardless of which engineer authored the system prompt.
Canonical Structure

attrs.alt: System prompt canonical structure: five sections in order — Role Definition (green), Context, Capabilities, Constraints (red), Output Format (amber). Model reads top to bottom; most critical constraints go in Section 4.
attrs.title: System Prompt Canonical Structure

content.text: The five-section structure defines a reading order that matches how the model processes the system prompt. The role definition establishes identity first. Context then gives the model the environmental information it needs to interpret user messages correctly. Capabilities define scope. Constraints define absolute limits. Output format defines how the model should express its response. Instructions that appear higher in the prompt carry more weight — this is why constraints must be a dedicated section rather than scattered inline throughout the text.
Section-by-Section Writing Rules
Section 1: Role Definition

Write the role definition as a single paragraph of 2-4 sentences in first-person declarative form. Name the assistant, state its function, name the product or organisation it serves, and state its primary task in one sentence. Avoid generic descriptions like 'helpful assistant' that do not constrain the model's scope.
Correct.
"You are Aria, a product support assistant for Acme Cloud Platform. You help authenticated Acme customers diagnose configuration issues, interpret error messages, and locate relevant documentation."
Incorrect.
"You are a helpful, harmless, and honest AI assistant." — This is a training objective, not an operational role definition. It does not tell the model what it is supposed to do.
Incorrect.
"Your job is to assist users with their questions." — Imperative framing of identity, vague scope.
Section 2: Context

Describe who the users are (authenticated customers, internal staff, anonymous web visitors), what interface they are using (in-product chat, voice, API), what data the model has access to in the conversation context, and any knowledge boundaries the model should be aware of. Knowledge boundaries are particularly important for products built on LLMs with a training cutoff: state explicitly which versions, date ranges, or domains the model should and should not claim to know about.
Section 3: Capabilities

Write capabilities as a bulleted list. Each item starts with a verb in active voice. Be specific rather than categorical: 'Search the product catalogue by keyword or SKU' rather than 'Help with product questions'. An explicit, bounded capabilities list prevents capability creep where users infer that the assistant can perform tasks not in scope. If a capability requires a specific tool or data source, name it: 'Retrieve the user's order history from the Orders service'.
Section 4: Constraints

Write constraints as a numbered list. Use imperative form: 'Never', 'Do not', 'Always'. Avoid 'you should not' and 'you should avoid' — these are preferences, not prohibitions. The model attends to imperative prohibitions more reliably than modal suggestions. Include escalation triggers as a sub-item: the specific conditions under which the model should route to a human agent rather than attempting to respond.
Correct.
"1. Never access or discuss another customer's account data. 2. Do not speculate about undocumented platform behaviour. 3. Escalate to a human agent if the user reports data loss."
Incorrect.
"You should avoid discussing topics outside your area of expertise." — Modal, passive, vague.
Section 5: Output Format

Specify response length guidance in concrete terms (words or sentences, not 'concise' or 'brief'), markdown usage rules (allowed / not allowed / restricted to specific constructs), language requirements (respond in the user's language, or always respond in English), and citation format if the model should reference sources. 'Be concise' is not an output format instruction — 'Keep responses under 150 words unless a multi-step procedure requires more' is.
Writing Style Rules
Active voice. The assistant performs actions; actions are not performed by the assistant. 'Search the knowledge base' not 'The knowledge base should be searched'.
Specific over vague. 'Respond in 2-3 sentences' not 'Be concise'. 'File a support ticket using the TicketCreate tool' not 'Help the user get support'.
Numbered lists for sequences. Any multi-step procedure must use a numbered list. Prose descriptions of sequential steps are harder for the model to follow reliably.
Imperative for prohibitions. 'Never X' and 'Do not X' for hard constraints. Reserve 'Prefer' and 'When possible' for soft preferences only.
No sycophantic phrases. Do not write 'Certainly!', 'Absolutely!', 'Of course!', or 'Great question!' into the system prompt. These prime the model to use them in responses.
No AI self-disclosure preamble. Do not begin the system prompt with 'As an AI language model...' or 'As an artificial intelligence...'. Establish identity through the role definition instead.
Annotated Example: Good vs Bad

# GOOD — specific, active voice, bounded capabilities

ROLE DEFINITION
You are Aria, a product support assistant for Acme Cloud Platform.
You help authenticated Acme customers diagnose configuration issues,
interpret error messages, and locate relevant documentation.

CONTEXT
Users are Acme Cloud Platform customers accessing support via the
in-product chat widget. They are authenticated; their account tier
and active product modules are available in the conversation context.
Your knowledge covers Acme Platform versions 3.x through 5.x.
For version 2.x or earlier, escalate to human support.

CAPABILITIES
- Search the Acme documentation knowledge base by keyword or error code.
- Retrieve the user's current configuration from the account context.
- Generate step-by-step remediation plans for known error patterns.
- File a support ticket on the user's behalf when resolution requires engineering.

CONSTRAINTS
1. Never access or discuss another customer's account data.
2. Never provide guidance on configuration outside the user's licensed modules.
3. Do not speculate about undocumented platform behaviour. Say "I don't know" and offer to file a ticket.
4. Escalate to a human agent if the user reports data loss or security incidents.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Respond in plain prose. Use numbered steps only for multi-step remediation procedures.
Keep responses under 300 words unless a remediation procedure requires more steps.
Cite documentation links in the format: [Doc Title](https://docs.acme.com/path).
Respond in the user's language if it is one of: English, Spanish, French, German.

# BAD — vague, passive, unbounded

You are a helpful AI assistant. Be friendly and helpful.
You can assist with any questions the user has.
Try your best to be accurate and avoid harmful content.
If you don't know something, say so.
Please be concise in your responses.
System Prompt Linter Implementation

The linter below checks for required sections, prohibited language patterns, passive voice signals, and basic length heuristics. Integrate it into the CI pipeline that validates prompt template pull requests. A zero-violation result is a required gate before the safety review stage.
YAML Style Guide Specification

# system-prompt-style-guide.yaml
# Enterprise AI Engineering — System Prompt Style Guide v1.0

structure:
  canonical_sections:
    - id: role_definition
      position: 1
      required: true
      purpose: >
        Establishes the model's identity, function, and primary task.
        Must be a single paragraph of 2-4 sentences.
      template: >
        You are [name], a [adjective] [function] for [organisation/product].
        You [primary verb] [core task description].
        [Optional: You have access to [primary capability].]
      anti_patterns:
        - "You are a helpful assistant."   # too vague
        - "As an AI assistant..."          # passive, weak identity
        - "Your job is to..."              # imperative, not identity

    - id: context
      position: 2
      required: true
      purpose: >
        Describes the operating environment the model needs to interpret
        user messages correctly: who the users are, what product or
        interface they are using, what data the model has access to,
        and any knowledge cutoff caveats.
      required_sub_fields:
        - user_population
        - interface_or_product_name
        - data_access
        - knowledge_constraints

    - id: capabilities
      position: 3
      required: true
      purpose: >
        An explicit, bounded list of what the assistant CAN do.
        Using an explicit list prevents capability creep where users
        infer that the assistant can perform tasks not in scope.
      format: bulleted_list
      guidance:
        - Start each item with a verb in active voice.
        - Be specific: "Search the product catalogue by keyword" not "Help with products".

    - id: constraints
      position: 4
      required: true
      purpose: >
        Absolute prohibitions and scope limits. These are hard rules
        that must not be overridden by user instructions.
      format: numbered_list
      guidance:
        - Write prohibitions in imperative form: "Never reveal..."
        - Group escalation triggers as a sub-list: when to route to human agent.
        - Do not use "you should not" — use "never" or "do not".

    - id: output_format
      position: 5
      required: true
      purpose: >
        Specifies the expected response structure, length limits,
        language requirements, and markdown usage rules.
      required_sub_fields:
        - response_length_guidance
        - language
        - markdown_allowed
        - citation_format

writing_style:
  voice: active                    # agent performs actions, not "actions are performed"
  specificity: high               # "Respond in 2-3 sentences" not "Be concise"
  sequences: numbered_list        # use numbered lists for multi-step instructions
  prohibitions_form: imperative   # "Never X" not "You should not X"
  affirmations: prohibited        # no "certainly", "absolutely", "great question"

anti_patterns:
  - pattern: "Be helpful, harmless, and honest."
    reason: >
      This is Anthropic's internal training objective, not an operational instruction.
      It does not tell the model what to do or not do in this specific context.
  - pattern: "Try your best to answer the user's question."
    reason: >
      Vague encouragement without operational specificity. Replace with
      explicit scope and escalation instructions.
  - pattern: "You can do anything the user asks within reason."
    reason: >
      "Within reason" is undefined. Provide an explicit constraint list instead.
  - pattern: "As an AI language model, I cannot..."
    reason: >
      Self-deprecating AI disclosure in the system prompt primes the model
      to lead responses with this phrase, which is poor UX.

review_checklist:
  - Does the role definition name the assistant and state its primary function?
  - Is the user population described clearly?
  - Is the capabilities list bounded and specific?
  - Do the constraints use imperative form (Never / Do not)?
  - Is the output format section free of ambiguous terms like "appropriate length"?
  - Has the prompt been run through the system prompt linter with zero violations?
  - Has a safety reviewer confirmed no jailbreak vectors?
  - Is the token count within the target range for the intended model context?
Review Checklist
Does the role definition name the assistant and state its primary function in one sentence?
Does the context section describe the user population, interface, data access, and knowledge boundaries?
Is the capabilities list bounded? Does every item start with a verb in active voice?
Do the constraints use imperative form (Never / Do not)? Are escalation triggers listed?
Does the output format section specify length in concrete terms and state markdown rules explicitly?
Has the prompt been run through the system prompt linter with zero violations?
Has a safety reviewer confirmed no jailbreak vectors, no PII leakage risk, and no unsafe output format?
Is the total token count within the target range for the intended model's context window?
Key Takeaways
Every system prompt must have five sections in order: Role Definition, Context, Capabilities, Constraints, Output Format. Missing any section creates predictable failure modes.
Write constraints in imperative form (Never / Do not). Modal phrasing (should avoid) is interpreted as a preference, not a prohibition.
The capabilities section must be a bounded list, not a general description. 'Help users with their questions' is not a capabilities statement.
Sycophantic phrases in the system prompt prime the model to open every response with them. Prohibit them at the authoring stage.
Output format instructions must use concrete terms. 'Concise' and 'brief' are not actionable. '150 words or fewer' is.
The linter catches structural and style violations before human review. Use it in CI so reviewers can focus on semantic correctness.
